title: Ranking Metrics: NDCG & MRR
concept: Normalized discounted cumulative gain and mean reciprocal rank as the standard ways to score a ranked list's quality.

exercises.prompt: In the last lesson, we learned about ranking approaches. What does a 'pairwise' approach do?

options.text: It scores each document independently of all others.
It compares pairs of documents to decide which one should rank higher.
It optimizes the entire ranked list of documents all at once.

exercises.explain: Pairwise ranking approaches take pairs of items and determine which one should be ranked higher, rather than scoring items individually (pointwise) or grading the entire list at once (listwise).

cards.title: Are the Best Results on Top?
cards.body: When you search for something online, you rarely look past the first few results. How do AI engineers mathematically prove that their search engine puts the absolute best links at the very top?

cards.title: Ranking Metrics Explained
cards.body: To measure search quality, we use MRR and NDCG. Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) measures how fast you find the first relevant result. Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG) measures the overall quality of the entire list, giving more points when highly relevant items appear near the top.

cards.title: The Grocery Shelf Audit
cards.body: Imagine organizing a search page like a grocery shelf. MRR only cares if the absolute best snack (first place) is at eye level. NDCG is like scoring the entire shelf, giving you points for every good item but penalizing you if top-tier snacks are hidden on the bottom shelf.

cards.title: Calculating the Scores
cards.body: If the first relevant link in a search is at position 3, its Reciprocal Rank is 1/3 (0.33). For NDCG, if your top 3 results are rated 'Excellent', 'Good', and 'Bad', NDCG calculates their weighted scores and compares them to the perfect order.

cards.title: The Perfect List Illusion
cards.body: You might think a high NDCG means every single search result is perfect. In reality, NDCG is normalized, meaning it compares your algorithm's order to the best possible order of those same items, even if the available pool of items is mediocre.

cards.title: MRR vs. NDCG
cards.body: Compare how MRR focuses on speed to find one item, while NDCG grades the placement quality of the entire list.

left.text: Scores only the first correct hit. Great for 'where is my passport' style queries.

right.text: Scores the entire list. Great for shopping searches with many good options.

exercises.prompt: Which ranking metric evaluates the relevance of the entire list of results, applying a discount to items placed lower down?
exercises.explain: NDCG stands for Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain. It measures ranking quality by discounting items that appear lower in the list.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If a search system places the best item at position 1, its Reciprocal Rank score is 1.0, even if all other results below it are completely irrelevant.
exercises.explain: MRR only cares about the first relevant result. If the first result is relevant, it scores a perfect 1.0, regardless of how bad the rest of the list is.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank: What ranking metric calculates 1 divided by the position of the first correct answer?
exercises.sentence: The Reciprocal Rank is 1 divided by the rank of the first correct result.
exercises.explain: Reciprocal Rank (RR) is calculated by taking 1 divided by the rank of the first relevant item. For example, if the first good item is at position 2, the RR is 1/2.

exercises.prompt: Identify the AI-generated statement about ranking metrics:

options.text: MRR is ideal for queries with a single target answer, like searching for a specific homepage.
NDCG uses a logarithmic discount to decrease the value of relevant items that are placed too low in the results.
We use the Mean Relative Discount (MRD) to find the average gains of all pages, which is then divided by the reciprocal value of the search.

exercises.explain: The third option is generated by an AI because it hallucinates 'Mean Relative Discount' which is not a standard ranking metric, and misdefines MRR as calculating average gains.

exercises.prompt: You are building a search engine for a library. If a user searches for 'Python', they get 5 relevant books. Why would you choose NDCG over MRR to evaluate this search results list?

options.text: MRR measures overall relevance across the whole list, while NDCG only tracks the single top result.
MRR only looks at the position of the first relevant result, while NDCG grades the quality of the entire list.
NDCG ignore positions entirely, while MRR heavily discounts lower positions using logarithms.
Both metrics ignore the order of results and only count the total number of relevant links.

exercises.explain: MRR only cares about the very first relevant item's position (1st item = 1/2). NDCG evaluates the whole list, applying a discount log-factor to all relevant items based on their position, making it perfect for multi-result evaluation.
